segments.s536-g33: چو here is the subordinating conjunction 'when' used in narrative. The phrase follows with the agent خربندگان ('the plunderers') and the object جامه‌های گلیم ('kilim garments') in the normal Persian order: conjunction + subject + object (a plural noun phrase). 'جامه‌های' is the plural possessive/partitive form 'garments of' and گلیم specifies the type (kilim).
segments.s536-g34: بپوشید is a 3rd-person plural past (narrative) form 'they put on / they wore' (the ب- prefix appears in epic narration). و is the coordinating 'and' linking the next noun phrase. بارش is composed of بار 'load' + enclitic ش 'his/its' (here 'their/its load'), meaning 'their load'; همه 'all' modifies the following goods, and زر و سیم is a simple coordination 'gold and silver'.
segments.s536-g35: همی is a narrative particle (here giving immediacy: 'then/so') combined with شد (past of شدن) to form 'they became.' The predicate is a compound nominal: خلیده‌دل (' withdrawn/retiring of heart') and راه‌جوی ('path-seeking, i.e. travelers/seekers'), joined by و. The subject is omitted but continues from the previous clause ('they/them').
segments.s536-g36: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/away from' and لشگر 'the army' is its object. سوی دژ 'toward the fortress' gives the direction. نهادند is the past (3rd pl.) of نهادن 'to set/put' and together with the following noun روی ('face') forms the light-verb construction نهادند روی — literally 'they set (their) face', idiomatically 'they turned (their) faces toward / they directed themselves toward.'
segments.s536-g37: همان is the demonstrative 'that/the same' pointing back to previously mentioned people. روستایی is a noun 'villager' (here used as a noun phrase) modified by the apposition دو مرد جوان 'two young men'; the order is demonstrative + noun + numeric/appositive description.
segments.s536-g38: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. بودند is the past-tense 'they were.' روزی here is an adverb meaning 'once/formerly' (poetic use). ورا is a dative/adverbial 'to them/there' referring back to the caravan, and میزبان is the predicate noun 'host.' The clause therefore reads 'who had once been host to them.'
segments.s536-g39: از آن انجمن 'from that assembly/company' uses از + demonstrative + noun. برد here is the past of بردن 'to take' (i.e. 'took'), followed by با خویشتن 'with himself' (با + reflexive خویشتن). Note: برد is ambiguous in the supplied lemma list (also meaning 'victory'), but in context it functions as the verb 'took (away).' The actor is the now-acting subject implied from context.
segments.s536-g40: که introduces an explanatory relative clause 'for/that' describing those taken: هم ... و هم is the correlative pair 'both ... and ...'. دوست 'friend' and رای‌زن 'counselor, adviser' are predicate nouns linked by بودند 'they were.' The clause states that the men were both friends and advisers.
segments.s536-g41: همی is a classical/perfective-prefix adverb giving a continuous or habitual sense ('kept on/was'), and رفت is the past of رفتن ('went'). همراه آن کاروان is an adverbial phrase: همراه here functions as 'with/together (with)', followed by the demonstrative آن and the noun کاروان as its object.
segments.s536-g42: به رسم = 'in the manner/according to the custom of'; به is used adverbially ('by/in'), رسم is 'manner/custom'. یکی مرد بازارگان is a noun phrase 'a certain man, a merchant' where یکی = 'a certain/one', مرد = 'man', بازارگان = 'merchant', describing the manner in which he traveled.
segments.s536-g43: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. از راه نزدیکی is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the road's nearness' (i.e. 'near the road'), where از marks source/position and نزدیکی = 'nearness'. دژ رسید: رسید is the past 'reached/arrived (at)', with دژ the place reached.
segments.s536-g44: This is a compound list of objects seen: دژ و باره و شهر ('the fortress and the bāre and the town'), followed by از دور دید where از دور = 'from afar' (از = 'from', دور = 'distant') and دید is the past 'saw/beheld'.
segments.s536-g45: پرستنده کرم is a nominative phrase 'one who praises/worships generosity' (پرستنده = 'admirer/worshipper', کرم = 'generosity'). بد شست مرد is an idiomatic/poetic phrase: بد here 'badly/ill', شست is past of شستن ('washed') used idiomatically to mean 'was treated/handled', and مرد = 'man' completes the subject phrase.
segments.s536-g46: نپرداختندی is an archaic/past negative plural meaning 'they did not pay' or 'did not attend/heed'. کس here in the scope of the negation functions as 'anyone/no one' (negative makes it 'no one'), از کارکرد = 'to/for his action(s)/deeds' where از = 'of/from' and کارکرد = 'performance/deed'.
segments.s536-g47: نگه کرد is a verbal compound 'looked' or 'gave a look' (نگه as noun/verb-stem 'gaze' with کرد 'did'). یک تن = 'one person/a man' (postposed subject), and به آواز گفت = 'spoke aloud' where به is adverbial 'by/in (voice)' and آواز = 'voice', گفت here functions as the past 'said/spoke'.
segments.s536-g48: This is a direct question introduced by که 'that/asking': صندوق را is the definite object ('the chest', with را marking the object), چیست = چی + ست 'what is', and اندر نهفت = 'hidden inside' (اندر = 'in/within', نهفت = 'hidden/concealed'), so the clause asks 'what is hidden inside the chest?'.
segments.s536-g9: چنین گفت پس شاه با پهلوان is arranged as a compact report clause: چنان(چنین) 'thus', گفت (past of گفتن) 'said', پس 'then/thereupon' gives a temporal sequence, شاه 'the king' is the subject, and با پهلوان 'with/to the champion' indicates the addressee (با here marks accompaniment/association and functions like 'to' in speech contexts). Word order is typical Persian: adverb/particle + verb + temporal marker + subject + complement.
segments.s536-g10: که ایدر همی باش روشن‌روان is the reported speech/command introduced by که (here functioning like 'that' or introducing the content). ایدر 'here' gives the place; همی is the archaic/prosaic progressive/emphatic particle used with verbs in Ferdowsi (marks continuative or emphatic imperative); باش is the imperative 'be/stay'; روشن‌روان is a vocative epithet 'bright‑soul' (compound adjective used to address the addressee).
segments.s536-g11: شب و روز کرده طلایه به پای is a complex verb phrase: شب و روز 'night and day' gives the time-frame, کرده is the perfect/participle of کردن used here in a causative/completive sense 'has made/placed', طلایه 'vanguard' is the object, and به پای literally 'at the foot of' (= in front of/at the head of) marks position relative to the riders. Word order places temporal adverbial first, then the verbal phrase and locative complement.
segments.s536-g12: سواران با دانش و رهنمای names those for whom the vanguard was placed: سواران 'the horsemen/riders' is the noun governed by the preceding locative، با 'with' introduces accompanying conditions or means, دانش 'knowledge/learning' and رهنمای 'a guide' are instruments or companions. The phrase as a whole indicates the vanguard served the mounted troops who had learning and guidance.
segments.s536-g13: همان دیده‌بان دار و هم پاسبان uses همان 'the same' to pick up a previous referent, دیده‌بان 'lookout/sentinel' names the role, دار here is part of a compound or attributive construction (classical usage where دار can indicate possession/holding) and هم پاسبان 'and also a guard' adds a second role. The phrase lists multiple duties of the same person; دار is an archaic element and its exact nuance is contextually distributive ('having/being of').
segments.s536-g14: نگهبان لشکر به‌روز و شبان names the function and the temporal span: نگهبان 'guardian/keeper' + لشکر 'of the army' states who is guarded, and به‌روز و شبان 'by day and by night' is a classical phrasing meaning 'both day and night' (به‌روز here 'of the day' i.e. daytime; شبان is an older/poetic form meaning 'nightly'/'at night' rather than the ordinary sense 'shepherd').
segments.s536-g15: من اکنون بسازم یکی کیمیا is a first‑person declarative with a future/intentional sense: من 'I', اکنون 'now', بسازم is the present/subjunctive/optative form of ساختن used here for 'I will make' (classical Persian often uses the present-subjunctive to express near future or intention), یکی 'one/a certain', کیمیا 'alchemy/elixir' is the object. The verb form is literary; the whole clause states the speaker's immediate plan.
segments.s536-g16: چو اسفندیار آنک بودم نیا is a comparative/relational clause: چو 'as/like' introduces the comparison, اسفندیار is the proper name, آنک (classical form of آن که) 'that which/who/that', بودم is past 'I was', and نیا here is an archaic word likely meaning 'ancestor' (or a vocative/kinship term). The clause is terse and poetically compressed: the speaker compares his elixir or state to Esfandiyar and then asserts a kinship/ancestral relation; the final word is somewhat archaic/ambiguous in sense.
segments.s536-g1: وزان is a compound particle here meaning 'from that (place/time)' and introduces the starting point. جایگه is the noun 'place'; شد is the past verb 'became/turned' used here to mean 'moved / turned (one's attention or course)'; سوی جنگ کرم is a prepositional phrase 'toward the battle of Karam' (سوی = toward, جنگ = battle). The phrase thus literally says 'from that place he turned toward the battle (of) Karam.'
segments.s536-g2: سپاهش is سپاه + possessive suffix ش = 'his army'. همی کرد is the classical continuous/habitual past construction (همی + کرد) meaning 'made / caused / set (continually)', here marking the action as purposeful and ongoing. آهنگ is a noun meaning 'intention/aim' and takes كرد as the light verb 'to make/set (an aim)'; کرم here names the target (proper name) so it functions as the object of آهنگ.
segments.s536-g3: بیاورد is the past tense 'he brought' (classical literary past). لشکر is the noun 'army/host'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and' (here linking numerals not listed individually in the word list); the whole segment lists the force he brought (literally: 'brought an army ten and two thousand').
segments.s536-g4: جهاندیده is an adjective 'worldly‑wise / experienced'; کارکرده is an adjective meaning 'having acted / experienced / battle‑tested' and intensifies the veteran quality; سوار is 'rider / horseman'. The sequence is a string of modifiers followed by the head noun, i.e. 'worldly‑wise and experienced horsemen.'
segments.s536-g5: پراگنده لشکر = 'the dispersed/scattered army' (پراگنده modifying لشکر). چو شد here uses چو 'when' + شد 'became/was' to form a temporal clause 'when ... became (so)' and همگروه (literally 'same‑group') means 'joined together/one company.' So the clause reads 'when the scattered army became joined/together.'
segments.s536-g6: بیاوردشان is بیاورد + object suffix شان = 'he brought them.' تا here expresses extent 'as far as / up to' (introducing the destination), میان = 'between' and دو کوه = 'two mountains.' Together: 'he brought them up to/between two mountains.'
segments.s536-g7: یکی here functions as 'a certain' (introducing someone). مرد بد نام = 'a man of bad name' i.e. 'ill‑famed' or 'infamous'. او is the 3rd‑person pronoun 'he'. شهرگیر is a descriptive title 'city‑taker' or 'besieger.' The phrase strings epithets and titles before the appositive that follows.
segments.s536-g8: خردمند سالار شاه اردشیر is a sequence of titles/appositions: خردمند 'wise', سالار 'commander/chief', شاه 'king', and اردشیر the proper name 'Ardashir.' In Persian the modifiers and titles precede or run in apposition to the name, so this nominative cluster identifies the man as 'the wise commander, King Ardashir.'
segments.s536-g49: چنین داد پاسخ بدو شهریار is a compact clause: چنين ('thus') fronted as an adverb, داد ('gave') is the simple past verb, and پاسخ بدو شهریار ('an answer to him, the king') is the object with an indirect-object phrase. بدو is the dative/indirect-object clitic phrase 'to him', and شهریار apposes/identifies that him as 'the king' (vocative/recipient), so the literal order is 'thus gave an answer to him, the king.'
segments.s536-g50: که introduces reported content ('that...'). هرگونه‌ای is a quantifier 'of every kind/any kind' modifying چیز ('thing'), which is the direct object. دارم is the present-tense possessive verb 'I have' (first-person), and به بار is a prepositional phrase indicating location/posing on the pack ('on/onto the load'); the clause means 'that I have all kinds of things on my pack.'
segments.s536-g51: ز here is the poetic preposition 'of/from' used to list contents. پیرایه, جامه, سیم, زر are coordinated nouns joined by و; the sequence names valuables (ornament, garment/robe, silver, gold). The connective و simply links each item in the enumerative list.
segments.s536-g52: Again ز introduces another group of goods. دینار (coin/money), دیبا (brocade/silk) and گهر (gem) are listed; و links them. Note: classical Persian poetic lists often use short nouns in succession without extra particles; 'در' in this phrase is a traditional word that in this context functions as a named item in the list (a kind of jewel/pearl) rather than the preposition.
segments.s536-g53: به بازارگانی خراسانیم: به + بازارگانی expresses involvement 'in/at trade/mercantry'; خراسانیم is a predicative/clausal ending that identifies the speaker as connected to Khorasan (i.e., a Khorasan merchant). The copular sense is carried by the -یم ending on خراسان, so the whole means 'I am a merchant of Khorasan' (classical first-person statement).
segments.s536-g54: به رنج اندرون بی تن‌آسانیم: به رنج 'by/with hardship' marks manner; اندرون 'within/inside' gives the sphere; بی is the negative particle 'without'; تن‌آسانیم is a nominal/compound meaning 'one who is easy-bodied / lives in comfort' — here negated, so 'I am not a man of ease.' The clause contrasts the speaker's hard life with luxury.
segments.s536-g55: بسی خواسته کردم از بخت کرم is an experiential perfect/simple past construction: بسی 'much/many' (here 'much'), خواسته (a nominalized/participle form meaning 'request' or 'thing asked for') plus کردم ('I made/did') yields 'I have asked for much' or 'I have begged much'. از بخت کرم is a genitive phrase 'from the generosity of fortune' (i.e., asking favors from fate/luck).
segments.s536-g56: کنون آمدم شاد تا تخت کرم: کنون 'now' + آمدم 'I came' (perfect/simple past first-person) + شاد 'joyful' describes the manner. تا here functions poetically like 'to/until' introducing the destination phrase تخت کرم 'the throne of generosity' (i.e. the king's seat of bounty). The whole means 'now I have come joyfully to the throne of generosity.'
segments.s536-g89: 'بخوردند' is past 3rd‑person plural (they ate/drank) and functions as the main past verb. 'می‌چند' is a present/imperfect form of 'چیدن' (they pluck/arrange) used in coordination with the past verb—poetic narrative can sequence actions with mixed aspect. 'و' simply coordinates the clauses. 'مستان شدند' is a light‑copular construction: 'مستان' (drunk ones) is the predicate nominal and 'شدند' (they became) is the past plural copula, so together 'they became drunk.'
segments.s536-g90: 'پرستندگان' is the plural noun 'worshippers' (subject/topic). 'می‌پرستان' is a compound/derived plural meaning those who worship or adore 'می' (wine) — here used as a label 'wine‑worshippers' (a lexicalized noun). 'شدند' is again the past 3rd‑person plural copula 'became' so the phrase means 'the worshippers became wine‑worshippers.' Word order is Subject — Predicate(Nominal) — Copula in poetic form.
segments.s536-g91: 'چو' is a temporal conjunction 'when'. 'از جام' is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of the cup'. 'می‌سست' is an imperfective/continuous past form meaning 'was breaking/loosening' (here of speech/tongue). 'شدشان' combines the past 'شد' (became/happened) with the clitic 'شان' ('their') — an archaic/poetic ordering putting the clitic before the noun — so together with 'زبان' ('tongue' / 'speech') it means 'their tongue became loosened' (i.e., their speech faltered/flowed because of drink).
segments.s536-g92: 'بیامد' is past 3rd‑person singular 'came'. 'جهاندار' is the agent noun 'sovereign/king'. 'با' is the simple preposition 'with' and 'میزبان' is 'host' (the person hosting/entertaining). The clause is straightforward: 'the sovereign came with the host.'
segments.s536-g93: بیاورد is a past (3rd person) form meaning “he brought/produced” (classical Persian word order often puts the verb up front). ارزیز is a direct object meaning “the precious (thing)” and is followed by و ‘and’ linking a second noun phrase. رویین is an adjective meaning “iron‑hard/adamantine” placed before the noun it modifies (رویین لوید = the iron‑hard Loid); لوید functions here as a proper name or noun. The clause therefore lists two brought items.
segments.s536-g94: برافروخت is a past (3rd person) verb meaning “kindled” (it can also mean “flushed/angered” but here it takes a literal sense). آتش is the direct object “fire.” به‌روز here is a genitive/attributive element meaning “of the day / of the daytime,” and سپید (literally “white”) is used adjectivally/poetically as “bright” or “clear” — together به‌روز سپید = “the bright day” or “daybreak.” Word order places the verb before its object phrase, typical of Ferdowsi’s style.
segments.s536-g95: چو is the temporal conjunction “when” (not simile here). آن is the demonstrative “that,” modifying کرم which here means “worm/grub” (not generosity). را is the definite/direct‑object marker attached to کرم (classical Persian often marks definite nouns this way). بود is the past form “was” and here functions existentially with the following noun phrase; گاه خورش = “a time/turn for the stew” (گاه = “time/turn,” خورش = “stew/dish”). The phrase literally reads “when to that grub there was a time for the stew.”
segments.s536-g96: ز is the preposition “from.” ارزیز is the same “precious” (here naming the precious dish or substance) and جوشان is a present‑participle/adjective “boiling,” forming “the boiling Arziz.” بدش is بد (“bad/poor”) plus the enclitic ش = “his/its,” so بدش پرورش = “his/its upbringing was bad/poor.” پرورش is the noun “upbringing/development.” The whole clause means that, from the boiling of the precious (dish), his nurture/development was poor.
segments.s536-g97: زبانش (زبان + -ش) is the possessed noun 'his tongue' (زبان = the organ 'tongue'; -ش = 'his'). بدیدند is a simple past, 3rd-person plural 'they saw'. همرنگ سنج is a postposed descriptive phrase: همرنگ = 'same-colored / of the same color' modifies سنج = 'scale (weighing‑scale)'; together they describe the tongue's color. Word order: object (زبانش) + verb (بدیدند) + predicate/adjective phrase (همرنگ سنج).
segments.s536-g98: بران‌سان is an adverbial simile ('forcibly / as if like a charger') qualifying the manner. که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/which/as', linking the simile to the following description. از پیش is a temporal phrase 'before / previously'. خوردی is an archaic poetic past form meaning 'had eaten' (from خوردن). برنج is the direct object 'rice'. Together the clause reads 'as if (بران‌سان) that had before eaten rice.'
segments.s536-g99: فرو ریخت is a compound verb: فرو (down/away) + ریخت (past of ریختن 'to fall/flow') giving 'fell down' / 'sank'. ارزیز is a substantive/adjective meaning 'precious / valuable' describing what fell (i.e., his valued quality). مرد جوان is a noun phrase 'the young man' (مرد = man/person; جوان = young/adult young man). The sequence means 'the young man's precious (worth/courage) fell.'
segments.s536-g100: به here marks direction or result 'into / to'. کنده is a participial noun 'the uprooted (stump/root)' and درون means 'inside / within'. کرم in this context is the abstract noun 'generosity / nobility'. شد (past of شدن) is the copulative 'became', and ناتوان is the predicate adjective 'powerless / incapable'. The phrase as a whole says 'into the uprooted inner part of his generosity he became powerless' (i.e., his generosity was torn out and he became powerless).
segments.s536-g101: تراکی (here) functions as the noun 'a cry/crying-out' (in the line it denotes the sound that issues). برآمد is the past 'arose / issued forth'. ز is the classical preposition 'from', حلقوم is 'throat', and اوی is the archaic possessive 'his/its'. Word order: [sound] + 'arose from' + his throat.
segments.s536-g102: که introduces a result/clause 'so that / and', linking the cry to its effect. لرزان شد is a compound where لرزان (trembling) + شد ('became') = 'became trembling' or 'trembled'. آن کنده و بوم اوی lists the things affected: آن کنده 'that stump' and بوم اوی 'his dwelling/abode' (بوم = dwelling/abode here; اوی = his). The coordinated nouns are the subjects that 'trembled.'
segments.s536-g103: بشد is the past of شدن here meaning 'he became / he moved' (used idiomatically as 'he rushed' or 'he joined in'). با جوانان is the prepositional phrase 'with the young men' (با = with; جوانان = youths/young men). چو = 'like' introduces the simile, باد = 'wind' or 'gale', and اردشیر is the proper name 'Ardashir', so the whole phrase means 'he moved with the young men like Ardashir's wind' (i.e., he rushed like Ardashir's gust).
segments.s536-g104: ابا here is a poetic form representing a prepositional sense 'with' (in the line it stands before the list of weapons), followed by a coordinated list: گرز 'mace/club', شمشیر 'sword', گوپال (a proper name or epithet; here presented as an item/name in the list), and تیر 'arrow'. The repeated و are simple conjunctions 'and'. Overall: 'with mace and sword and Gupal and arrow.'
segments.s536-g65: Structure: subject 'یکی' (an indefinite person) + object phrase 'سفره پیش پرستندگان' + verb. 'یکی' here means 'one/someone' (indefinite subject). 'سفره' is the spread/banquet being set; 'پیش' here means 'in the presence of / before' and governs the following noun 'پرستندگان' (worshippers). Word order is typical Persian: subject – object – verb.
segments.s536-g66: 'بگسترد' is an older/perhaps poetically prefixed past-form meaning 'he/one spread' (verb after object). The conjunction 'و' links a second verb 'برخاست' (past: rose/stood up). 'چون' introduces a simile 'like/as', and 'بندگان' here means 'servants' — the whole clause says he rose in the manner of servants.
segments.s536-g67: 'ز' (poetic 'از') marks source: 'from the chest'. 'بگشاد' is an older past form meaning 'he opened' (the chest is the implied object). The phrase then lists the items brought out: 'بند' (a band/lock) and 'کلید' (key), connected by 'و'.
segments.s536-g68: 'برآورد' here functions as a verb 'brought forth/produced' (classical usage), followed by 'و' and a second verb 'برداشت' meaning 'he lifted/took up'. The direct object 'جام نبید' is 'a cup of wine' (جام = cup, نبید = wine). The two verbs are coordinated actions referring to producing the cup and taking it up.
segments.s536-g69: 'هرانکس که' = 'whoever'. 'زی' is the archaic/prepositional form equivalent to 'از' meaning 'from/of' (here: 'by/through'), so 'زی کرم بردی' = 'received by (your) generosity' (بردی is past 'took/received'). 'خورش' is the cooked dish / food item received. The clause says: anyone who received a dish through generosity.
segments.s536-g70: 'ز' again 'from/of' introducing the ingredients 'شیر و برنج' = 'milk and rice'. 'آنچ' is a relative/ demonstrative 'that which/what', followed by 'بد' (bad) modifying 'پرورش' (upbringing/ breeding). The phrase 'آنچ بد پرورش' = 'that which bespeaks/comes from bad upbringing' (no copula is expressed).
segments.s536-g71: 'بپیچید' is a classical/perfective form meaning 'they twisted/turned' (here in narrative past). 'گردن' is 'neck' and 'ز' = 'from/away from' marks the source; 'جام نبید' = 'the cup of wine' is the thing they turned away from.
segments.s536-g72: 'که' introduces the reason/explanation clause. 'نوبت بدش' = 'his bad turn/fate' (نوبت = turn; 'بد' = bad; '-ش' = his/its). 'جای مستی ندید' literally 'saw no place for drunkenness' — idiomatically 'left no room/opportunity for being drunk'. 'ندید' is past negative 'did not see' used figuratively.
segments.s536-g25: بسی (here) functions as an intensive/adverbial meaning abundant or many; it precedes the noun it modifies. گوهر 'gem/jewel' is the direct object, introduced by از 'from' which marks source ('from the treasure'). بگزید is the past tense verb 'chose/selected'. نیز at the end links this action to additional items mentioned next ('likewise'). Word order is subject omitted — typical Persian poetry — with object + verb.
segments.s536-g26: ز is the poetic form of 'از' marking source ('from'). دیبا 'brocade/silk' and دینار 'dinar/coins' are coordinated by و 'and'. هرگونه چیز is a compound meaning 'every kind of thing' (هرگونه = any kind, چیز = thing). This whole phrase completes the 'نیز' from the previous segment: he likewise selected these items.
segments.s536-g27: به چشم خرد is a prepositional phrase 'in the sight of wisdom' (چشم = eye/sight; خرد = wisdom) — a possessive/construct relationship. چیز ناچیز کرد is a verb phrase where چیز is the direct object and ناچیز is an object-complement meaning 'insignificant'; کرد is the past tense 'made/did', so literally 'he made the thing insignificant (in the eyes of the wise)'.
segments.s536-g28: دو صندوق is 'two chests' (numeral + noun). پر here is the adjective 'full (of)' and takes the following noun as its contents: پرِ سرب = 'full of lead'. The coordination 'سرب و ارزیز' links lead and 'valuables/precious things' (ارزیز). کرد again is 'he made/filled' — so the clause means 'he filled two chests with lead and valuables.'
segments.s536-g29: یکی = 'one' or 'a certain' introduces the item. دیگ رویین is a noun + postpositive adjective: 'an iron (رویین) cauldron (دیگ)'. به بار اندرون is a locative/relational phrase 'as part of the load inside' (به بار = as/for the load; اندرون = inside). The phrase lists this cauldron as part of the interior's load.
segments.s536-g30: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('for' / 'because'). استاد بود او is an inverted predicate: استاد بود = 'he was a master/expert' with the subject او 'he' placed after the verb, a poetic ordering. به کار اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'in the work/affairs of the inner quarters' specifying the domain of his mastery.
segments.s536-g31: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. از بردنی is a prepositional/adjectival phrase where بردنی (formed from بردن 'to carry' + -نی) means 'for carrying' or 'portable'. جامه‌ها is the plural object 'the garments'. کرد راست is past tense 'made' (کرد) + راست used adverbially 'properly/ready' or 'straight' — together meaning 'he made (them) ready to be carried.' Word order places the 'for carrying' qualifier before the noun.
segments.s536-g32: ز marks source/agent 'from' or 'of'. سالار آخر = 'the last commander/lord' (آخر postmodifies سالار as 'last'). خری here takes the sense 'donkey' (خری as the animal) rather than 'to buy'; بخواست is past 'he requested/asked/desired'. The clause therefore means 'he asked the last commander for a donkey' (source marked by ز).
segments.s536-g73: چو (conjunction) = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. بشنید is a past tense 3rd‑person singular verb 'he heard.' The verb جست ('leapt') follows the prepositional phrase بر پای ('to/on (his) feet'), and اردشیر (the proper name) appears after the verb by poetic inversion — the normal order would be اردشیر جست بر پای (Ardashir sprang to his feet).
segments.s536-g74: که here works as a causal conjunction 'for/because.' با من is the prepositional phrase 'with me.' فراوان modifies the following noun phrase as 'abundant/plentiful.' برنجست is a poetic/archaic nominal/verb form referring to rice/grain (here taken as 'staple food'), and the coordinating و links شیر ('milk') as the second item in the list of provisions.
segments.s536-g75: به + noun here expresses agency/means 'by/at the order of.' دستوری functions as 'order/command' and is followed by سرپرستان ('the supervisors/chiefs') in an older poetic sequence (no modern ezāfe), while سه روز is a temporal phrase 'for three days.'
segments.s536-g76: This line uses archaic word order and an infinitive to express the feeding action. مر is the dative 'to me' (archaic form of مرا), او را is the definite object 'him,' and بخوردن is the infinitive 'to eat/to feed' functioning as the action to be carried out. منم is emphatic 'I am' and is followed by the epithet/name دلفروز — i.e., 'he was to be fed to me; I am Dolfrooz.'
segments.s536-g77: مگر here reads as an concessive/rhetorical 'might I not / unless' opening an optative/exclamatory idea. شوم is the present-subjunctive 'may I become' (1st person), and در جهان شهره‌ای is the predicate 'famous/renowned in the world' with شهره‌ای a substantive/adjectival noun 'a renown/fame.'
segments.s536-g78: This clause expresses an optative wish. م (here representing archaic مرا split) + را correspond to 'for me' (mora → م + را) and is followed by باشد, an optative/subjunctive 'may there be.' از introduces the source 'from,' اخترش is 'its star' (اختر + possessive ‑ش), and بهره‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a share/portion.' The construction literally: 'May there be for me a share from its star.'
segments.s536-g79: شما is the 2nd‑person plural/formal subject 'you.' می‌گسارید is present indicative 2nd‑person plural (poetic verb 'to spend/scatter/consume'); با من سه روز is a prepositional temporal phrase 'with me for three days' placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s536-g80: چهارم is an ordinal/time adverb 'on the fourth (day).' چو is the comparative/temporal 'as/when/like,' introducing the simile. خورشید گیتی فروز is a compact adjectival/predicative phrase 'sun, world‑illuminating' (فروز as 'illuminating'/'making bright'), implying 'on the fourth I will, like the sun, illuminate the world.' The subject (I) is carried over from the preceding clauses.
segments.s536-g57: اگر introduces a conditional/content clause ('if' / 'that it is the case that'). بر پرستش فزایم is an older Persian clause where فزایم (first‑person singular present/subjunctive of فزودن) means 'I increase' and the prepositional phrase بر پرستش = 'in/to worship' precedes the verb. رواست is the predicate ('it is fitting/appropriate'), with -ست acting as the copula; the clause literally reads 'If that I increase in worship, it is fitting.'
segments.s536-g58: که introduces the reason ('because/that'). از بخت او is a prepositional phrase 'by/from his fortune' (از here = 'because of / by means of'), followed by the subject کار من 'my affair(s)' and the past verb گشت 'became/turned'. راست is an adjectival predicate 'right/true' placed after the verb. The clause therefore gives the cause: 'because by his fortune my affairs became right.'
segments.s536-g59: پرستنده is an agent noun 'the worshipper/devotee' and کرم follows as a modifying noun 'of generosity' (a noun‑noun modification common in older Persian). بگشاد is a past verb 'opened/revealed' (گشود + ب‑), and راز is the direct object 'secret'. Word order is flexible/poetic: subject (پرستنده کرم) + verb (بگشاد) + object (راز).
segments.s536-g60: هم‌انگه is an adverb meaning 'at that moment' or 'immediately then'. در دژ is a locative phrase 'in/at the fortress' (در = in/at, دژ = fortress/citadel). گشادند is the third‑person plural past of گشودن ('they opened' or 'were opened'), implying plural subject (the gates) that is not stated here, and باز is an adjective/adverb 'open' (i.e. became open). Together: 'at that moment the fortress (its gates) were opened.'
segments.s536-g61: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آن بار is the noun phrase 'that load/burden'. او راند is 'he drove' with the pronoun او before the past verb راند (a normal older Persian ordering), and اندر حصار is the locative 'into/within the fortress' (اندر = in/within, حصار = fortress). The line reads 'When he drove that load into the fortress...'
segments.s536-g62: بیاراست is the past form of بیاراستن 'to adorn/prepare', here 'he adorned/prepared'. کار از در is a genitive/partitive phrase 'the work of/from the gate' (از = of/regarding, در = door/gate), and نامدار is an adjective 'renowned' modifying the noun (most naturally 'the famed work' or 'the gate, famed'). The clause means 'the famed one prepared the work at the gate' (subject is inferred from context).
segments.s536-g63: سر بار is a compound noun 'the head/top of the load' (سر = top/head, بار = load). بگشاد is the past verb 'opened'. زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying the action, and اردشیر is the proper name 'Ardashir' (subject). The ordering reflects older poetic syntax: object phrase + verb + adverb + subject yields 'Ardashir quickly opened the top of the load.'
segments.s536-g64: ببخشید is the past third‑person of بخشیدن and here means 'he gave/bestowed' (contextual, not 'forgave'). چیزی is the indefinite object 'something'. که introduces a relative clause 'that/which'; بد = 'bad/evil/misfortune' and ز + و/او (poetic clitic) yield 'from it/him' (i.e. 'from that misfortune'), and گزیر is a noun 'escape/way out'. The relative clause therefore reads 'which gave escape from misfortune' (i.e. 'he gave something that provided an escape from misfortune').
segments.s536-g81: برآید is a 3rd-person present/optative-like verbal form here meaning 'if it comes about / if it be possible'; it introduces a conditional/time clause. یکی کلبه سازم is a clause with یکی as the indefinite 'a/certain', کلبه as the noun 'cottage' and سازم (ساخت + ـم) the 1st-person present/future 'I will build / I build', so the verb is first-person while the conditional marker (برآید) precedes it. فراخ is a postpositive adjective modifying کلبه (classical/poetic word order: noun followed by adjective).
segments.s536-g82: سر طاق is a noun compound meaning 'summit/roof of the vault' or 'vaulted top'; برتر ز is a comparative phrase 'higher/above than' where برتر is the comparative adjective and ز (poetic for از) introduces the standard of comparison. ایوان و کاخ are the nouns compared against. This whole phrase apposes/describes the cottage just mentioned.
segments.s536-g83: فروشنده‌ام is a noun + 1st-person enclitic ـم meaning 'I am a seller' (predicate nominal with no separate copula). هم here means 'also / even' and links to the following participial adjective خریدارجوی 'seeking buyers' (a present/active-form adjective), so the clause says the speaker both sells and actively looks for purchasers.
segments.s536-g84: فزاید is 3rd-person present 'it/this increases' or 'adds to'; the object 'مرا' (split in the tokenization as م + را) follows the verb in poetic order (i.e. 'increases me' = 'adds to my ...'). نزد + کرم is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the generous', and آبروی is the abstract noun 'reputation / honour' that is increased. Overall the clause says that this fact increases the speaker's reputation with the generous.
segments.s536-g85: برآمد is a past/immediate perfective 'arose / came about'; همه کام او is the noun phrase 'all his desire' (همه quantifies کام, او is the postnominal possessive 'his'). زین سخن is a prepositional phrase 'from this speech' (classical contraction of ز + این) placed after the verb to indicate source.
segments.s536-g86: بگفتند is past tense 'they said' introducing direct speech; the following clause is an interrogative with inverted/poetic word order: کو را = 'whom' (interrogative + object marker را), تو is the 2nd-person subject 'you', and کن is the 2nd-person imperative 'do' so the phrase literally reads 'Whom, you do worship?' (= 'Whom will you worship?').
segments.s536-g87: برآورد here is a past 3rd-sing. verb meaning 'took on / assumed / put forth' (poetic perfective); خربنده (an older/dialectal form of خریدار) is the subject 'the buyer'. هرگونه رنگ is the object phrase 'every kind of appearance/guise' (هرگونه modifies رنگ). The verb precedes its object in this classical order.
segments.s536-g88: پرستنده بنشست is a simple past-clause 'the worshipper sat down' (بنشست = past 'sat'). با introduces the accompaniment 'with', and می‌به چنگ are two nouns coordinated by juxtaposition meaning 'wine (mi-ye beh / poetic wine-term) and a harp'; thus 'sat down with wine and a harp.'
segments.s536-g105: پرستندگان را: 'پرستندگان' (worshippers) is fronted and followed by the enclitic 'را' used here as a topical/emphatic marker. 'که بودند مست' is a relative clause introduced by 'که' (who/that) with the past copula 'بودند' (they were) and the adjective 'مست' (drunk) as the predicate; word order (noun + را + که + verb + adjective) is poetic but grammatically marks 'the worshippers' as the topic and then describes them as intoxicated.
segments.s536-g106: یکی زنده از تیغ ایشان نجست: 'یکی' (one/a single person) is used in the sense of 'not one' with the negated verb. 'زنده' (alive) is an adverbial/predicative adjective qualifying survival. 'از تیغ ایشان' is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of their sword' (instrument/agent), with 'ایشان' the archaic 3rd‑person plural 'their'. The verb 'نجست' is past of جَست with the negative prefix ن‑, so here it means 'did not leap/escape' → 'did not survive/escaped not' (i.e. no one escaped alive).
segments.s536-g107: برانگیخت از بام دژ تیره دود: 'برانگیخت' is a past intransitive verb here meaning 'rose/was raised'. The spatial source is given by 'از بام دژ' = 'from the roof (بام) of the fortress (دژ)' (genitive/linking order is poetic). 'تیره دود' is 'dark smoke' — the adjective 'تیره' placed before 'دود' for poetic emphasis.
segments.s536-g108: دلیری به سالار لشکر نمود: 'دلیری' (bravery/valor) is the thing shown; 'به سالار لشکر' is a prepositional phrase 'to/before the commander of the army' (سالار = commander, لشکر = army). 'نمود' is the past of 'نمودن' here meaning 'showed/displayed'. The object comes before the prepositional phrase and the verb follows, a normal poetic order.
segments.s536-g109: دوان دیده‌بان شد بر شهرگیر: 'دوان' is a verbal form meaning 'they ran/rushed' (used adverbially/verbally to indicate swift motion). 'دیده‌بان شد' literally 'became a watchman' or more idiomatically 'the watchman was in motion/appeared' — together the phrase conveys 'the sentinels rushed forth'. 'بر شهرگیر' is 'against/on the besieger' with 'بر' = toward/against and 'شهرگیر' = besieger.
segments.s536-g110: که پیروزگر گشت شاه اردشیر: 'که' introduces the clause ('so that/and') or a relative/explanatory connection. 'پیروزگر گشت' = 'became victorious' (پیروزگر = victor/conqueror; گشت = became). The subject 'شاه اردشیر' (King Ardashir) follows the predicate, an inverted order used for emphasis.
segments.s536-g111: بیامد سبک پهلوان با سپاه: 'بیامد' is the past 'he came/arrived'. 'سبک پهلوان' is an epithet 'the light/nimble champion' (سبک here = light/nimble used adjectivally before پهلوان in poetic order). 'با سپاه' is the accompaniment 'with the army/troops'.
segments.s536-g112: بیاورد لشکر به نزدیک شاه: 'بیاورد' is a past-tense verb meaning 'he brought' (here continuing the narrative). 'لشکر' is the direct object 'the army/host' and 'به نزدیک شاه' is the goal phrase 'to near the king' (i.e. he brought the force up close to the king). Word order places the verb then object then prepositional goal in this poetic line.
segments.s536-g17: اگر is the conditional particle 'if'. دیده‌بان is the subject 'the watchman/sentinel' and دود the direct object 'smoke'; بیند is an archaic present-form of دیدن (used like modern ببیند) meaning 'sees' — here marking the protasis of the conditional. به‌روز is an adverbial phrase 'by day / during the day', placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s536-g18: شب 'at night' is the temporal adverbial starting the apodosis. آتش is the subject 'fire'; چو is the simile particle 'like/as'; خورشید is the comparison-term 'the sun'. گیتی فروز is a verbal/adjectival expression: فروز (used here as a present/predicative stem 'illuminates/sets aglow') takes گیتی as its object — 'illuminates the world'. The line uses poetic inversion but reads 'at night fire, like the sun, lights the world.'
segments.s536-g19: بدانید is an exhortative 'know that' or 'take notice that'. کامد is an archaic present-form meaning 'comes/has come' (modern می‌آید or آمده), and the phrase به سر کار 'to the head/top of affairs' is an adverbial phrase indicating position; کرم is the nominative/predicate 'generosity' — 'know that generosity has come to rule the affairs.'
segments.s536-g20: گذشت is the past verb 'passed' (or 'has passed'); اختر و روز 'star and day' is a poetic way to indicate a time/season; بازار کرم is a genitive phrase 'the market of generosity' (بازار modified by کرم). Word order is verb then its subject/phrase for poetic effect: 'the star and day of generosity's market passed.'
segments.s536-g21: گزین کرد is a compound past ('he chose' — گزین as the root + کرد as the past auxiliary). زان is the preposition 'from those/from that', مهتران 'the great ones/chiefs' (poetic plural), and هفت مرد is the numeral phrase 'seven men' (i.e. seven heroes) as the object of انتخاب/گزین.
segments.s536-g22: دلیران و شیران are two appositive epithets identifying those chosen — 'the brave ones and the lion-like (i.e. valiant men)'. روز نبرد is a temporal/occasional phrase 'for the day of battle' (روز 'day' modified by نبرد 'battle'). The nouns are plural descriptive epithets; the sequence names the men and the occasion.
segments.s536-g23: هرآنکس 'whoever/anyone who' plus the relative particle که introduces the subordinate clause. بودی is an archaic past-form 'was' used here in a generalizing sense. هم‌آواز is an adjectival/compound noun 'in unison; companion in song' and اوی is the archaic clitic referring to 'his' or 'him' — together 'whoever was his like-minded companion.'
segments.s536-g24: نگفتی is an older-form verbal negation roughly 'did not tell/reveal' (poetic/archaic conjugation). به باد هوا is a fixed-like phrase 'to the wind of whim/desire' (باد = 'wind', هوا = 'whim/desire' — i.e. the fickle air), راز 'secret', and اوی again 'his' — together: 'did not tell his secret to the capricious wind.'

sentences.s536-s17: When the plunderers put on kilim garments and (took) all their load of gold and silver...
sentences.s536-s18: They became withdrawn of heart and way-seeking, and turning from the army they set their faces toward the fortress.
sentences.s536-s19: Those same villagers — two young men — who had once been their hosts there.
sentences.s536-s20: From that company he took with him (two men), for they were both friends and counselors.
sentences.s536-s21: He went along with that caravan, in the manner of a certain merchant.
sentences.s536-s22: When he came near the road to the fortress, he saw the fortress, the bāre, and the town from afar.
sentences.s536-s23: The man who praised generosity was ill rewarded; no one paid heed to his deeds.
sentences.s536-s24: One man looked and, calling aloud, said, 'What is hidden inside the chest?'
sentences.s536-s5: Thus the king said to the champion: “Stay here, bright‑soul.”
sentences.s536-s6: He posted a vanguard day and night at the front of the horsemen, with knowledge and a guide.
sentences.s536-s7: He was the same lookout and also the guard, the keeper of the host by day and by night.
sentences.s536-s8: I will now make one elixir, like Esfandiyar — for I was of that lineage (ancestor).
sentences.s536-s1: From that place he turned toward the battle of Karam; he made his army set its course toward Karam.
sentences.s536-s2: He brought an army of twelve thousand worldly‑wise, battle‑tested horsemen.
sentences.s536-s3: When the scattered host had been gathered together, he brought them into the space between two mountains.
sentences.s536-s4: A certain ill‑famed man, a city‑taker — the wise commander, King Ardashir.
sentences.s536-s25: He replied to the king thus: "I have all kinds of things on my pack."
sentences.s536-s26: Of ornaments and garments and silver and gold; of dinars and brocade and pearls and gems.
sentences.s536-s27: I am (engaged) in the merchantry of Khorasan; by hardship I dwell within and am not one who lives easily.
sentences.s536-s28: I have asked a great deal of fortune's generosity; now I have come joyful to the throne of generosity.
sentences.s536-s45: They ate and plucked, and became drunk; the worshippers turned into wine‑worshippers.
sentences.s536-s46: When their tongues began to loosen from the cup, the sovereign came with the host.
sentences.s536-s47: He brought forth Arziz and the iron‑hard Loid, and kindled the fire of the bright day.
sentences.s536-s48: When that grub had its turn at the stew, from the boiling Arziz his upbringing was poor.
sentences.s536-s49: They saw his tongue the color of a scale, as if—like a charger—it had eaten rice before.
sentences.s536-s50: The young man's precious worth sank down; the inner root of his generosity was torn out and he became powerless.
sentences.s536-s51: A cry rose from his throat, and that stump and his dwelling trembled.
sentences.s536-s52: He surged with the young men like Ardashir's gale, with mace and sword and Gupal and arrow.
sentences.s536-s33: One spread a table before the worshippers and rose up like a servant.
sentences.s536-s34: He opened the chest and brought out the lock and key, and then produced and lifted a cup of wine.
sentences.s536-s35: Whoever, by your bounty, received a dish of milk and rice — that which betrays poor upbringing.
sentences.s536-s36: They turned their necks away from the cup of wine, for his bad turn allowed no place for drunkenness.
sentences.s536-s13: He selected many gems from the treasure, likewise (from) brocade, dinars, and every kind of thing.
sentences.s536-s14: In the eyes of the wise he made the thing seem insignificant; he filled two chests with lead and valuables.
sentences.s536-s15: One iron cauldron among the load inside, for he was a master in the work of the inner quarters.
sentences.s536-s16: When he had made the garments ready to be carried, he asked the last commander for a donkey.
sentences.s536-s37: When Ardashir heard it he sprang to his feet, for there was with me plenty of rice and milk.
sentences.s536-s38: By order of the supervisors, for three days he was to be fed to me; I am Dolfrooz.
sentences.s536-s39: May I become renowned in the world; may there be for me a share of its star.
sentences.s536-s40: You will spend three days with me; on the fourth, like the sun, I will light up the world.
sentences.s536-s29: If it is fitting that I increase my devotion, it is because, thanks to his fortune, my affairs have turned out right.
sentences.s536-s30: The devotee revealed the secret of generosity; at that moment the gates in the fortress became open.
sentences.s536-s31: When he drove that load into the fortress, the renowned one prepared/appointed the work at the gate.
sentences.s536-s32: Ardashir quickly opened the top of the load; he gave something that provided escape from misfortune.
sentences.s536-s41: If the chance arises, I will build a spacious cottage whose vaulted summit rises above the iwan and palace.
sentences.s536-s42: I am a seller and I even seek buyers; that adds to my standing before the generous.
sentences.s536-s43: All his desire arose from that speech; they said, 'Whom will you worship?'
sentences.s536-s44: The buyer assumed every kind of guise; the worshipper sat down with wine and a harp.
sentences.s536-s53: The worshippers, who were drunk — not a single person escaped alive from their sword.
sentences.s536-s54: Dark smoke rose from the fortress roof; he displayed bravery before the army's commander.
sentences.s536-s55: The watchmen rushed against the besieger; King Ardashir became victorious.
sentences.s536-s56: The nimble champion came with the troops and brought the army close to the king.
sentences.s536-s9: If the watchman sees smoke by day, at night fire will blaze like the sun and illumine the world.
sentences.s536-s10: Know that generosity has come to the head of affairs; the star and the day of the market of generosity have passed.
sentences.s536-s11: From those chiefs he chose seven men, brave and lion-like, for the day of battle.
sentences.s536-s12: Whoever was his companion in song did not reveal his secret to the capricious wind.
